I grew up speaking French as my first language, but I quickly adapted to life in America. I was born in New York, just after my parents arrived in the country. By the time I graduated high school in 1943, the world was engulfed in conflict, with news of battles and struggles echoing through the streets. Rationing was a part of daily life, and the sense of uncertainty loomed large over everyone. I was fortunate enough to be accepted into Harvard that June, and I remember the excitement of starting my college journey amidst the backdrop of sacrifice and resilience that defined those times.

I initially planned to pursue a degree in a technical field focused on the design and development of processes that convert raw materials into valuable products at the University of Chattanooga and then transfer to Georgia Tech. However, when I entered my freshman chemistry class in February 1946, I found myself in a class of 94 students, most of whom aspired to become engineers or doctors. I decided to change my major to chemistry, where I could delve into the study of substances, their properties, and the reactions that transform them. I graduated in just two years and eight months, finishing in September 1948.

The cold was the worst part, seeping into your bones until you couldn't tell if you were shivering from fear or just the damn weather. Sometimes, late at night, I can still hear the echoes of gunfire and the distant rumble of artillery in my dreams.

I remember the smell of burnt coffee and stale bread hanging in the air, no matter the time of day. When the atomic bomb was eventually dropped on Japan, I had mixed feelings. I understood the gravity of the situation; it brought an end to a bloody conflict that could have escalated further. It was estimated that hundreds of thousands of lives would have been lost if the war continued, both among our military personnel and Japanese civilians. I felt a sense of relief that my brother, who was in the Army and on his way to the Pacific, was spared from what could have been a devastating deployment. The ship he was on turned around after the bombs were dropped, and he returned home instead of facing the horrors of war. The city that was struck was known for its beautiful coastal views and vibrant port, but in an instant, it became a scene of unimaginable destruction, with its historic buildings reduced to rubble and countless lives forever altered.

During the Battle of the Bulge, we found ourselves near Eupen, Belgium, and had to retreat about 40 kilometers to secure our supplies. On Christmas Day of 1944, something remarkable occurred. We were welcomed into homes adorned with simple decorations, where the warmth of the hearth contrasted with the chill outside. Families, with their faces lined by hardship yet glowing with generosity, invited us to share in their holiday meal. We were taken aback by the kindness shown to us, especially given the circumstances. Although we didn’t know what kind of meat we were eating, it was a warm meal shared with good people who offered smiles and laughter, creating a moment of joy during a difficult time.

On Christmas Day of 1944, something remarkable happened. The local residents invited us to their homes for Christmas dinner, which was a humbling experience. We were taken aback by their kindness, especially considering the circumstances. We didn’t know what kind of meat we were eating, but it was a warm meal shared with good people during a difficult time.

The stillness would settle, a heavy blanket muffling the sounds of the jungle. You could taste the humidity, thick and green, and feel the prickle of sweat beading on your skin as you waited. Every rustle became a threat then, every shadow a potential enemy.

I remember boot camp like it was yesterday, though some parts I wish I could forget. Sharing close quarters with so many others, it wasn't long before the itching started. Tried everything to get rid of the critters, but they were persistent little devils.

The briefings were intense, maps spread out, the air thick with cigarette smoke and nervous energy. I remember the knot in my stomach tightening with each passing hour, the weight of responsibility settling on my young shoulders. We knew what was coming, the day etched in our minds, a line drawn in the sand.

We were out on an outpost when the stern figures in their crisp uniforms arrived, and I felt a wave of anxiety wash over me. They were searching for a young soldier, and I knew that such authoritative personnel don’t come to the front lines unless it’s serious. With their badges gleaming and a no-nonsense demeanor, they called out his name, and I held my breath, wondering if they would call mine next. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. Their presence was commanding, and as they escorted him off, leaving me behind, I felt a mix of relief and concern.

It was a much-needed break, and I felt grateful to experience a moment of joy amidst the turmoil.

The streets were packed, wall-to-wall with people celebrating like I'd never seen before. I remember thinking, "Finally, maybe I can go home and see my girl." Everyone was kissing and hugging, strangers even, just overcome with joy that the fighting in Europe was done.

Eventually, the rotation began, and I was on the first supply vehicle back to my unit. I returned to the same company, but things had changed. I had been the squad leader before I got hit, but now the BA Airman had taken my place, and I found myself in a role I hadn’t been trained for. The familiar faces of my comrades were still there, but the dynamics felt different; the camaraderie that had once defined our small group was now tinged with uncertainty. I could see the new airman leading the team through drills and coordinating our missions, while I struggled to find my footing among the tight-knit group I once led.

text: While some in the German community may have felt conflicted during the wars, my family was always patriotic. We never harbored any sentiments for the German homeland, especially not for the Nazi regime. When I traveled back to Germany with friends from high school, we visited the towns where their ancestors lived. They found their names on historical rolls, and I realized that I had no such connection. My father’s great-grandfather came from Germany, but I never felt the need to trace that lineage. After two world wars, my identity is firmly rooted in the values of freedom, diversity, and opportunity that define my life today. I embrace the spirit of resilience and the belief in the pursuit of happiness, which shape my understanding of who I am.

After my service, I wasn't sure what I'd do, but Uncle Sam had my back. That little piece of paper opened up a whole new world of possibilities I never thought I'd get. Suddenly, college wasn't just for the wealthy anymore.

Back then, they called us up from all over the state, seemed like overnight. Suddenly, I was trading my surfboard for a rifle, heading south near the border, though it wasn't what I signed up for, not at all. We were just kids, really, trying to keep the peace, caught between two worlds.

I could smell the sulfur from the burning phosphorus, and I recalled the training that taught us to get air away from it to stop the flames. The only solution I could think of was to jump into the water. I noticed Private Burley, an older soldier with a weathered face and a gaze that spoke of countless battles fought long ago, already in the water, so I stood up to leap from the boat. Just then, something struck me in the back, distracting me. I was holding my rifle with one hand and grabbed the side of the boat, but my grip slipped, and I began to go under.

We spent about 11 months in England, from July of '43 until June of '44, and then we prepared to move to France. We landed on Omaha Beach on D-13, which was actually June 18th. Thankfully, we didn’t have to fight our way ashore; the infantry and combat engineers had already secured the beachhead. It was a small area, and I remember thinking how easily we could have been pushed back into the sea if the disciplined and well-equipped forces opposing us had chosen to counterattack. Their reputation for strategic planning and fierce resistance was well-known, and our leaders were well-prepared, allowing us to feel fortunate to have their guidance.

I wouldn’t say I played a lot, but we managed to get together at least once a month while in North Africa. The USO would send orchestrations for various instruments, and I was grateful for the music. We had a trumpet player, a guitarist, and another friend who played the tenor sax. It was a nice distraction from the realities of war, and we played tunes that were popular back home. The Red Cross would even bring us doughnuts and coffee before missions, although I often joked that they were really meant for the pilots. My friend, who hailed from a bustling city known for its iconic car factories and rich musical heritage, was particularly frustrated by this, but I didn’t mind too much; I wasn’t a big fan of coffee anyway.

text: Growing up in New York City, I was immersed in the vibrant culture of the city, but my roots were European. My parents were immigrants; my father hailed from France, known for its rich history of art, cuisine, and philosophy, while my mother was from Switzerland. I grew up speaking a melodic language that was central to my family's heritage, filled with nuances and expressions that reflected a deep appreciation for culture and tradition, but I quickly adapted to life in America. I was born in New York, just after my parents arrived in the country. By the time I graduated high school in 1943, the war had already been raging for two years. I was fortunate enough to be accepted into Harvard that June, and I remember the excitement of starting my college journey.

text: Once I completed the 16 weeks of basic training, I was given 30 days to go home before being deployed. During that time, I had to go through an interview where they assessed my skills. They told me I had two options: I could become an equipment operator or a mechanic. I had no experience in either field, and I was adamant that I didn’t want to pursue those paths. When I pressed them for alternatives, they mentioned a position related to operations in the Asia-Pacific region, specifically involving coordination and support for military activities. I had no idea what that entailed, so I asked, "What does that involve?" They explained it was a role based in Japan, focusing on strategic initiatives, logistics, and collaboration with allied forces in a region known for its complex geopolitical landscape.

When I pressed them for alternatives, they mentioned something called FECOM. I had no idea what that was, so I asked, "What is a FECOM?" They explained it was the Far Eastern Command, and it was based in Japan.

text: During that time, we encountered a patrol of Germans, but they were quickly eliminated. The sounds of explosions and the relentless staccato of rapid-fire rounds filled the air, a constant reminder of the conflict surrounding us. One day, while marching through the woods with my captain, we were ordered to spread out as we approached a town under attack. Suddenly, we found ourselves pinned down in an open field by a hail of bullets that sliced through the air with a menacing whine from two concealed positions. We took cover in a ditch filled with icy water, which was better than being exposed to enemy fire. We called for tank support to help us locate the source of the onslaught, and eventually, we managed to assault the town.
origin: During that time, we encountered a patrol of Germans, but they were quickly eliminated. The sounds of artillery and machine gun fire filled the air, a constant reminder of the conflict surrounding us. One day, while marching through the woods with my captain, we were ordered to spread out as we approached a town under attack. Suddenly, we found ourselves pinned down in an open field by crossfire from two machine guns. We took cover in a ditch filled with icy water, which was better than being exposed to enemy fire. We called for tank support to help us locate the machine gunners, and eventually, we managed to assault the town.

Throughout my time in England, I forged lasting friendships with the guys in my outfit. We’ve been having reunions since 1974, and the last one was right here in Chattanooga. I even had the privilege of buying caps for each of our veterans, which proudly display the 752nd Engineers emblem. Our first reunion in a charming town known for its rich music heritage and southern hospitality had about 150 people, including wives. Over the years, we’ve gathered in various locations like Kingsport, Nashville, Memphis, Gatlinburg, and even a city in Ohio where our company commander hailed from. The warmth of the people and the beautiful rolling hills always make our gatherings special. We share a strong camaraderie, and it’s always a joy to reconnect with old friends. Just the other day, I ran into one of our men at a meeting, and it’s moments like that that remind me of the bonds we formed during the war.

The transition was swift. I found myself at a sprawling military installation in the heart of Missouri, surrounded by vast training fields and imposing barracks, immersed in basic training. Those 10 to 12 weeks were a whirlwind of experiences. I remember the shock of it all—the constant sounds of drill sergeants barking orders, the discipline enforced by strict schedules, and the intensity of physical training sessions. I was in good physical shape, which helped me keep up, but adjusting to the rigorous routines and the camaraderie of fellow recruits was a challenge. It was a different world, one that demanded respect and obedience, and I had to learn quickly how to navigate the unique environment of military life.

The living conditions improved slightly at Camp Evans. The hooches we stayed in were better, and the hospital setup was similar to what we had before, but the food situation was notably improved. Supplies came in more easily, and I couldn’t quite put my finger on why, but it felt like we were better supported there. Unlike at Quang Tri, where we had to coordinate with Marines and the Fifth Mechanized Infantry, the unit at Camp Evans had a clear understanding of the challenges we faced. They were seasoned veterans, known for their airborne operations and rapid deployment capabilities, and they had experience in the Ashau Valley, fully aware of the stakes involved. Their commitment to excellence and readiness ensured we were well-equipped and supplied, making a significant difference in our operations.

As I settled into my role in the military, there was an underlying tension in the world that we all felt. We knew that the situation in Korea could escalate at any moment, and Europe was no different. The atmosphere was charged with a sense of rivalry and suspicion, as nations eyed each other warily, each side building up its defenses and stockpiling weapons. Our unit was always prepared for action, ready to respond to any provocation. Despite the uncertainty, I found my time in active duty to be some of the happiest years of my life. It was peacetime, and while nobody was shooting at me, the camaraderie among my fellow soldiers was palpable. Everyone was congenial and friendly, united by a shared purpose, and we took our jobs seriously, especially the officers and the dedicated enlisted men who were career army personnel, committed to maintaining stability in a world on edge.

In 1968, my unit was called to go to Vietnam, and I was appointed as the officer in charge of the security police for a prominent air wing known for its strategic operations and vital support roles. We had everything prepared for deployment, and I had even arranged for my family to rotate visits while I was stationed at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, a hub for advanced military logistics and training. However, the night before I was set to leave, I received a call from Colonel Holbert, who informed me that I wouldn’t need to go to active duty because we had enough security personnel already deployed. I was relieved and felt no bitterness about the situation; I understood how quickly things could change.

It’s funny how life works out. If it hadn’t been for World War II, I would have never met my wife, Mary Ellen. She was in the Army Nurse Corps, and although she signed up, her name wasn’t drawn for overseas duty. Instead, she was stationed at a large military medical facility in Cleveland that had been transformed from a bustling army hospital during the war, complete with surgical wards and recovery rooms filled with veterans. Two of her close friends were sent overseas, but she remained behind, and that’s how our paths crossed.

This area, often shrouded in mist and echoing with the sounds of gunfire, would soon gain notoriety for its fierce battles and heavy casualties. We were moved to Camp Evans around Christmas of 1968, and I remember that time vividly. A few of us in the company were fortunate enough to get tickets to see the Bob Hope show in Da Nang. It was a much-needed break from the harsh realities of war, and I felt grateful to experience a moment of joy amidst the turmoil.

I remember Pa's farm well; endless fields of gold under the summer sun. Ma always said the air smelled different out there, cleaner, somehow sweeter than anything you'd find in town. Even now, I can almost feel the dirt between my toes and hear the crickets chirping as the sun went down.

We were out on an outpost when the stern figures in their crisp uniforms arrived, and I immediately felt a sense of unease. Their authoritative presence and the way they moved with purpose signaled that they were searching for a young soldier, and I knew that such visits to the front lines meant something serious was afoot. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if mine would be next. Thankfully, it wasn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. With a firm grip, they led him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

text: The Korean winters were brutal, but at least I had a warm tent. Back then, I was in charge of keeping the radios humming, making sure the boys could call for backup if they needed it. It was a lonely job sometimes, but knowing I was their lifeline made it worthwhile.

The air was thick with the smell of lemons and diesel, a strange mix that always takes me back. I remember the sun beating down on the cobblestones, so hot you could fry an egg on them, and the sound of the sea always nearby. Even now, I can almost taste the granita I used to get from that little stand by the harbor.

Dust devils danced across the Kansas plains as we marched, a sea of doughboys in ill-fitting uniforms. They said we were building character, toughening us up for what was to come overseas. Funny thing is, the real battle was against the boredom and the ever-present prairie wind.

We were surrounded, pinned down, no hope for reinforcements. Radio crackled, then went silent. All we could do was wait for the order that would seal our fate, coming from somewhere far behind their lines, a place we imagined reeking of cigar smoke and cold calculation.

I remember the smell of their starched shirts and new leather shoes, so different from the mud and diesel we were used to. Their questions felt like picking at a scab, never quite understanding the heat of the moment.

The humidity was something else, and the food, well, it certainly woke up your taste buds. I remember writing letters home, trying to describe the temples and the markets, but words just couldn't capture it. Some of the guys never came back from there, chasing ghosts in the jungle.

The scent of pine and hops always takes me back. We spent some time there after the war, supposedly keeping the peace, but mostly we were just kids in a fairytale land of cuckoo clocks and endless beer gardens. I still have a chipped stein somewhere, a souvenir from those days.

After a brief time at Chu Lai, I was quickly sent further north, taking a C-130 to Da Nang and then a helicopter to my new destination. The flight was an eye-opener. As we flew over the rugged landscape, dotted with lush greenery and scarred by the remnants of conflict, I was struck by the reality of the situation. I had never felt so vulnerable in my life, and the sights below were both breathtaking and terrifying. The area was marked by its proximity to the demilitarized zone, a stark reminder of the ongoing war, with military installations and the occasional convoy of troops visible from above. I was with a group of fellow outsiders, all of us sharing the same mix of apprehension and determination. I had received orders to the 18th Surgical MUST hospital, but I had no idea what a MUST hospital even was.

text: The heat there was something fierce, baking the very ground you walked on. We were supposed to be supporting the push north, but mostly we just waited, hoping for a letter from home and praying the next shipment of fuel wouldn't get lost. Funny the things you remember, like the taste of the local wine, strong and rough, but welcome after a long day.

I still remember that one time back in '57, we swapped out Sergeant Peterson's coffee with prune juice. He nearly blew a gasket when he took that first sip, sputtering and yelling something fierce. Good times, good times, even if we did have to scrub the latrines for a week straight after that stunt.

The late nights are a blur now, fueled by lukewarm coffee and the fluorescent hum of the library. I remember the weight of those stacks, the smell of aged paper, and the quiet satisfaction of finally piecing it all together, like a puzzle only I could solve. Funny, after all that, how little it seems to matter now, compared to a warm cup of tea and the laughter of my grandkids.

I remember spending so many hours practicing drill, the rifle heavy in my hands, the commands echoing across the quad. The crisp uniforms always made us stand out on campus back then, a brotherhood forged in discipline and precision. That training stayed with me long after I hung up the uniform, teaching me the value of teamwork.

In that bustling city known for its iconic Space Needle and vibrant waterfront, I had some duties to perform—washing dishes, cleaning the barracks, and whatever else was needed until the rest of the men arrived. The air was often crisp and filled with the scent of coffee from the nearby cafés, and the sound of rain was a familiar backdrop. I didn’t mind; I was just looking forward to the adventure ahead. The excitement of heading to Japan filled me with anticipation, even though there was nothing happening at that time. It felt like a new chapter was about to begin, and I was ready to embrace it.

The program lasted another 10 to 12 weeks, focusing on emergency medical care, trauma management, and life-saving techniques in high-pressure situations. I dove into the rigorous training with determination, learning how to assess injuries, administer first aid, and provide critical support to fellow soldiers in the heat of battle.

Our first gathering in Dyersburg, Tennessee, brought together about 150 people, including wives, and was filled with heartfelt reminiscing and camaraderie. The patrol consisted of nine men, and we loaded into a small wooden boat—what I called a "duck boat"—with four paddles. My squad and Sergeant Rice were in the boat as we started paddling across the wide, flowing expanse of water, the current gently pushing against us. Just as we reached the halfway point, enemy machine guns opened fire on us from the opposite shore, where the trees lined the bank and the sound of rushing water echoed around us. We scrambled to return fire, but in the chaos, a tracer round hit the rivet in the boat and struck me in the right leg.

We were out on an outpost when the MPs arrived, and I immediately felt a sense of unease. They were searching for someone, and I knew that MPs don’t come to the front lines unless it’s serious. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if they would call mine next. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was too young. He was barely out of his teens, with a boyish face that still held traces of innocence, and he often fumbled with his gear, a sign of inexperience. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

My job as a control tower operator was both demanding and rewarding. We had two main areas of responsibility: the control center, where we managed flight plans and reported incoming and outgoing aircraft, and the control tower itself, where we directed the traffic in the vicinity of the runways and taxiways. It was crucial to maintain a controlled environment, ensuring that planes took off and landed safely while avoiding collisions on the ground. We did this through radio communication with the pilots. The pilots were young, mostly in their early twenties, and they approached their training with a serious demeanor. Their lives depended on their focus and skill, and I felt a sense of responsibility in ensuring their safety as they navigated the complex network of paths and zones.

We spent 11 months there preparing engineer echelon sets for combat engineer battalions and aviation battalions in anticipation of the critical operations that would soon unfold. The atmosphere was charged with urgency as we approached the summer of that pivotal year, when the Allied forces would launch a massive offensive to reclaim occupied territories. These sets were essential supplies, packed in boxes with various parts that would be needed in the field, ready for the moment when the tide would turn.

We spent 11 months there before the invasion in June of '44, preparing engineer echelon sets for combat engineer battalions and aviation battalions. These sets were essential supplies, packed in boxes with various parts that would be needed in the field.

text: I spent the rest of my time at Camp Evans, and as the months passed, I found myself contemplating my future. By August, I made the decision to extend my service. I didn’t want to return home just yet; the thought of going back to the mundane life I had known in Seattle felt unappealing. I had experienced so much in that vibrant, chaotic land filled with lush jungles, bustling markets, and the constant hum of life around me. The camaraderie forged in the heat of challenging days and the thrill of navigating a world so different from my own made it hard to leave. I wasn’t ready to abandon the connections I had built and the purpose I had found. I wanted to stay engaged, to continue serving in a way that felt meaningful, rather than slipping back into the routine I had once found boring.

After our time in Paris, we moved into Belgium, where I remember a place called Modave. It was around this period that a fierce winter offensive began, marked by heavy snow and freezing temperatures. We faced some harrowing moments, including being bombed and strafed by American planes that had been commandeered by German pilots. The sound of artillery echoed through the valleys, and the air was thick with tension. We would scramble for cover under Fruehauf trailers whenever the planes came in low, our hearts racing as we braced for the next wave of chaos.

We had lingered too long, and we ended up near several expansive, still bodies of water, their surfaces reflecting the sky, surrounded by rugged terrain and steep banks. While the Marines received headlines for their struggles there, we were engaged in intense fighting at other locations, and we lost almost our entire outfit.

After landing on Omaha Beach, we were integrated into a larger military operation known for its strategic planning and coordination among various units, but as enlisted men, we didn’t have the full picture of where we were headed next. I remember moving from Omaha to places like La Capelle and Saint-Lô, where the atmosphere was charged with urgency and determination. I can still recall the day when the Air Force bombed Saint-Lô for hours, wave after wave of bombers coming in to clear the way for the infantry and ground troops, all part of a relentless push to secure the region and advance our objectives.

I can still vividly recall my first day of training. It was an experience unlike anything I had ever encountered before—cultural shock is the term that comes to mind. I arrived at Hunter College in New York, where the Waves and Marines prepared together, but it was strictly a women’s program at that time. The atmosphere was charged with determination and camaraderie, filled with the sounds of drills and the sight of uniforms. We were pushed to our limits, both physically and mentally, as we learned discipline, teamwork, and resilience. I was surrounded by other women who shared my commitment to serve, and together we faced the challenges that would forge us into capable and confident individuals.

My first assignment took me to the transportation office of Basic Class No. 14 in a bustling military installation in Virginia, known for its extensive training facilities and rich history in logistics and transportation. I met some remarkable gentlemen there, and the school was excellent. I remember how Eisenhower had just signed a bill requiring us to pass the course to maintain our commissions. It was a wake-up call for some in my class who didn’t take it seriously; they lost their commissions, which reinforced the importance of hard work and dedication. The atmosphere was filled with the sounds of drills and the sight of soldiers in uniform, all dedicated to mastering the art of military transport.

After the war, I returned home on a troop ship, and nothing could compare to the sight of the towering figure holding a torch high above her head as we approached the harbor. Her majestic presence, a beacon of hope and freedom, filled me with a mix of emotions—relief, joy, and a sense of accomplishment. My husband had flown home ahead of me, and we reunited in Long Island before making our way to his ranch in Los Gatos, California. We had a beautiful home and raised quarter horses, but our plans for a big family were altered when Jack was injured during the war. We eventually adopted two wonderful children, a boy and a girl, and I found joy in being a mother and later a grandmother.

text: I remember arriving on a ship, seeing her silhouette in the distance. The green glow against the morning sky, a promise of hope and a new life in this country. It was a sight that made my heart swell with the possibilities that lay ahead.

The salt spray stung my face as I clung to the railing, each wave a cold fist punching the bow. Nights were the worst, the endless black swallowing everything but the churning water and the groaning of the ship. I still feel that icy dread sometimes, even now, so many years later.
